context: ...we can read of that in 1 Chronicles 5 as the genealogy is given...
point_summary: Reuben's forfeiture of his firstborn birthright is confirmed in the genealogical record of 1 Chronicles 5, where the lineage does not follow him as firstborn.

context: In 1 Chronicles 5 and verse 26, we'll note that passage of scripture in 1 Chronicles 5, and it's sad because we can read with regard to the, of course, the nation of Israel going into captivity and the nation of Judah going into captivity. But here in 1 Chronicles 5 and verse 26, it begins to record for us the captivity of the two and a half tribes that are on the east side of the Jordan. It's really recorded in verses 23 through 26, and in verse number 26 it says, or verse 25, it says, they transgressed against the God of their fathers and went a whoring after the gods of the people of the land whom God destroyed before them. The God of Israel stirred up the spirit of Paul, king of Assyria, and the spirit of Tilgath Pilneser, king of Assyria, and he carried them away, even, notice the Reubenites
point_summary: Reuben's tribe, which first settled east of the Jordan for convenience rather than God's full inheritance, became the first carried into Assyrian captivity—fulfilling Jacob's prophecy that Reuben would not excel.

context: First Chronicles chapter 22. If you have a bulletin with you, you see that we're preaching from this passage.
point_summary: Preparation for God's house—gathering materials, workers, and resources—illustrates that faithful service involves deliberate, abundant readiness before the work of building begins.

context: In First Chronicles chapter 22, we find here beginning in verse number one, notice the Bible says, then David said, this is the house of the Lord God and this is the altar of the burnt offering for Israel.
point_summary: David's declaration in verse 1—identifying the threshing floor as the site for the house of the Lord and the altar of burnt offering—establishes the divinely appointed location that drives all of David's subsequent preparations for the temple.

context: And David commanded to gather together the strangers that were in the land of Israel and he sent masons of hue rock stones to build the house of God.
point_summary: David's command to gather the strangers in Israel illustrates that preparation for God's house required mobilizing every available resource and worker toward a singular, God-appointed purpose.

context: Verse three, and David prepared iron in abundance for the nails for the doors of the gates and for the joinings and brass in abundance without weight and cedar trees in abundance for the Zidaneans and they of Tyre brought much cedar wood to David.
point_summary: David's meticulous stockpiling of iron, brass, and cedar before his death illustrates the principle that faithful preparation for God's work is itself an act of devotion.

context: And David said, Solomon my son is young and tender. The house that is to be built for the Lord must be exceeding magnificent. I like that terminology. Of fame and of glory throughout all countries. I will therefore now make preparation for it. So David prepared abundantly before his death.
point_summary: David's description of the temple as "exceeding magnificent" in verse 5 establishes the standard of excellence that drove his abundant preparation before his death.

context: Then he called for Solomon his son and charged him to build a house for the Lord God of Israel.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: just a few chapters earlier in 1 Chronicles 17, God provides us with a record of David's desire to build the temple
point_summary: David's desire to build the temple, though redirected by God through Nathan, establishes the background to 1 Chronicles 22 by showing that God's plan, not man's intention, determines who does His work.

context: ...we look at the background really to the text that we're looking at this morning in 1 Chronicles 22...
point_summary: David's willingness to submit when God redirected his good desire to build the temple establishes the background principle that God's plan must take priority over even commendable human intentions.

context: ...there are some valuable lessons for us from the thought of 1 Chronicles 17...
point_summary: Though David's desire to build the temple was commendable, God's refusal of it establishes that even good desires must yield to God's sovereign plan, providing the essential backdrop to 1 Chronicles 22.

context: As we think about this text in 1 Chronicles 22 and fast forward from that desire that David had and the plan of God being revealed, I want you to consider with me this morning the importance of David's communication to Solomon as it's contained here in chapter 22 of the book of 1 Chronicles.
point_summary: Though David was denied the privilege of building the temple, he submitted to God's plan by preparing abundantly for Solomon's work rather than withdrawing in disappointment.

context: then he, David, called for Solomon his son and charged him to do what? To build a house for the Lord God of Israel.
point_summary: David's calling of Solomon in verse 6 marks the pivot of the chapter, where the gathered assembly recedes and the personal charge from father to son—to build the house of God—becomes the passage's central focus.

context: And David said to Solomon, My son, ask for me. It was in my mind to build a house unto the name of the Lord my God.
point_summary: David's heartfelt desire to build God's house was denied not from indifference but because God disqualified him on account of the blood he had shed in war.

context: But the word of the Lord came to me, saying, Thou hast shed blood abundantly and hast made great wars. Thou shalt not build a house unto my name, because Thou hast shed much blood on the earth in my sight.
point_summary: David's disqualification from building the temple is grounded in his being a man of bloodshed, with God reserving that honor for Solomon, the man of rest and peace.

context: Behold, a son shall be born to thee, who shall be a man of rest. And I, God says, I will give him rest from all his enemies round about. Notice this, for his name shall be Solomon,
point_summary: God's choice of Solomon as temple-builder was grounded in divine promise—God himself declared Solomon would be a man of rest, that peace would mark his reign, and that he would build the house for God's name.

context: Remember the significance of the communication here between David and Solomon in 1 Chronicles 22.
point_summary: David's charge to Solomon in 1 Chronicles 22 frames the temple-building task as God's revealed will, requiring Solomon's personal recognition of that calling and dependent trust in God's wisdom and strength.

context: And so how he responds to that which he recognizes in chapter 22 of 1 Chronicles is of significant importance.
point_summary: Knowing God's will creates moral accountability, so that Solomon's "determined" response in 2 Chronicles 2:1 illustrates that recognizing divine responsibility demands firm resolution, not half-hearted intention.

context: Remember it was David in 1st Chronicles 22 that told him he had prepared, but that he also would be able to prepare and thou mayest add thereto according to the last statement of verse number 14 of 1st Chronicles 22.
point_summary: David's charge that Solomon could "add thereto" to the prepared resources (v. 14) is cited as evidence that Solomon's understanding, displayed in 2 Chronicles 2, was precisely what God had equipped him to exercise.

context: one of the statements that he made was that only the Lord give thee wisdom and understanding in verse number 12.
point_summary: David's charge that God would give Solomon wisdom and understanding (v. 12) is seen fulfilled in Solomon's discerning, capable response to the building task assigned him.

context: David in 1st Chronicles 17 he had in his heart. I'm gonna build a temple
point_summary: David's prior desire to build the temple (1 Chr. 17) illustrates that God's communicated plan creates accountability — Solomon was responsible precisely because David faithfully passed that revealed will on to him.

context: This portion of Scripture, the charge from David, the King of Israel, to his son.
point_summary: David's charge to Solomon in 1 Chronicles 22:16—"Arise, therefore, and be doing"—is treated as carrying the same imperative force as Christ's Great Commission in Matthew 28:19, calling believers to active gospel witness.

context: Notice back in the Old Testament, if you will, 1 Chronicles 29, I love the way that this particular portion of Scripture is the wording here. And what a joy when you think about the greatness and the power of our God. And in 1 Chronicles 29, in verse number 11, just a powerful verse of Scripture,
point_summary: God's absolute sovereignty and victory over all earthly opposition is grounded in the declaration that greatness, power, glory, victory, and majesty belong entirely to the Lord.

context: The Bible says in 1 Chronicles 29, in verse number 11, Thine, O Lord, is the greatness and the power and the glory and the victory and the majesty for all that is in the heaven and in the earth is Thine. Thine is the kingdom, O Lord, and Thou art exalted as head above all.
point_summary: David's doxology in 1 Chronicles 29:11 is cited as Old Testament testimony that greatness, power, glory, victory, and majesty belong entirely to God, who reigns as head above all—grounding Christ's resurrection victory in the character of God himself.

context: of Zebulun, such as went forth to battle, expert in war, with all instruments of war, 50,000, which could keep rank, then notice what it says, they were not of double heart.
point_summary: Zebulun's men are commended in 1 Chronicles 12:33 as battle-ready, expert warriors fifty thousand strong who "were not of double heart"—a mark of singular loyalty and integrity.

context: moreover, they that were nigh them, even unto Issachar and Zebulun and Naphtali, brought bread on asses and on camels and on mules and on oxen and meat, meal, cakes of figs and bunches of raisins and wine and oil and oxen and sheep abundantly, for there was joy in Israel.
point_summary: 1 Chronicles 12:40 is cited to illustrate that Zebulun's descendants functioned as merchants and suppliers, bringing abundant provisions on pack animals, confirming Jacob's prophecy of their commercial, trade-oriented identity.

context: when we look at this truth in 1 Chronicles 12, when the Bible says there were men of understanding of the times
point_summary: The men of Issachar in 1 Chronicles 12, who had "understanding of the times," serve as a model for believers today to be informed rather than fearful, grounding that understanding in the whole canon of Scripture God has revealed.

context: in 1 Chronicles chapters 2 through 10, there's a chronological order that is given of the tribes, and you'll notice that in that portion of Scripture that Dan also is missing from there.
point_summary: The tribe of Dan's omission from the tribal genealogies in 1 Chronicles 2–10 parallels its absence in Revelation 7, reinforcing the prophetic significance of Jacob's warning about Dan's idolatry.

context: the writer of the book of 1 Chronicles names Achan as the troubler of Israel
point_summary: The writer of 1 Chronicles names Achan "the troubler of Israel," illustrating that secret sin—seen, desired, taken, and hidden—brings lasting, defining judgment.

context: First Chronicles deals with that as well, as you look at the chronological events that are going on with Reuben, and that blessing being given to Ephraim and Manasseh, the sons of Joseph, you can look at First Chronicles chapter five, and see that there as well.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we find in First Chronicles chapter 16 the record is given here to us of that in which David is praising the Lord
point_summary: David's act of bringing the ark back to Jerusalem, recorded in 1 Chronicles 16, is presented as a biblical example of the deliberate, practiced discipline of praising God in all circumstances.

reference_text: 15th chapter of the book of First Chronicles
context: heart to bring the ark of God back to Jerusalem and if you look through the passage of Scripture and you look at the 15th chapter of the book of First Chronicles you'll find some things that happen
point_summary: In 1 Chronicles 15, Israel's correction of their earlier failure to transport the ark according to God's prescribed instructions illustrates that worship and praise must be offered in the way God has ordained.

context: in fact back in chapter 13 you read about Uzzah and the the death and so on and so forth
point_summary: The death of Uzzah in 1 Chronicles 13 is cited as the consequence of Israel's failure to transport the ark according to God's prescribed instructions, contrasting with the corrected obedience that enabled worship in chapter 16.

context: so they brought the ark of God and set it in the midst of the tent that David had pitched for it and they offered burnt sacrifices and peace offerings before God
point_summary: After Israel corrected its failure to transport the ark according to God's prescribed order, David's first act upon its arrival in Jerusalem was to offer sacrifices and deliver a psalm of thanksgiving to the Lord.

context: then on that day David delivered first this psalm to thank the Lord into the hand of Asaph and his brethren
point_summary: On the day the ark was successfully returned to Jerusalem, David's first act was to deliver a psalm of thanksgiving — establishing gratitude to God as the fitting, immediate response to his faithfulness.

context: give thanks unto the Lord call upon his name make known his deeds among the people
point_summary: Gratitude expressed through calling on God's name and proclaiming His deeds publicly is presented as the fitting response to the ark's restoration to Jerusalem under David's corrected, obedient worship.

context: sing unto him sing psalms unto him talk ye of all his wondrous works
point_summary: Singing praise and recounting God's wondrous works is presented as the fitting response when the ark—God's presence—is restored to its rightful place among His people.

context: glory in his holy name let the heart of them rejoice that seek the Lord
point_summary: Glorying in God's holy name is presented as the fitting response of those who seek the Lord, grounded in David's psalm of thanksgiving when the ark was restored to Jerusalem.

context: seek the Lord in his strength seek his face continually
point_summary: Seeking God's face continually (v. 11) is set within David's psalm of thanksgiving at the ark's return, framing persistent pursuit of God as integral to a life of gratitude.

context: for great is the Lord and greatly to be praised
point_summary: God's greatness and incomparable worthiness of praise are grounded in the contrast between the Lord who made the heavens and the false gods of the nations, which are mere idols.

context: also is to be feared above all gods for all the gods of the people are idols but the Lord made the heavens
point_summary: God's greatness and incomparability over all false gods is the ground David's psalm gives for the praise and thanksgiving offered when the ark was restored to Jerusalem.

context: give unto the Lord the glory due unto his name bring an offering and come before him worship the Lord in the beauty of holiness
point_summary: Giving glory due to God's name, expressed through offering and worship in the beauty of holiness, is presented as the fitting response to His greatness on a momentous occasion of praise.

context: Oh give thanks unto the Lord for he is good for his mercy endureth forever
point_summary: Gratitude for God's goodness and enduring mercy, voiced at a momentous occasion of restored worship, is presented as the pattern and priority of a people who purpose to praise the Lord.

context: and we find here in first Chronicles 16 that when this on this momentous occasion when the ark was brought back to Jerusalem
point_summary: David's deliberate, purposeful praise on the momentous occasion of the ark's return to Jerusalem illustrates that God's people are to actively and intentionally exalt, thank, and glorify the Lord.

context: The life of David near the end of his life... the response of David and the people with the ability to give to the work of God.
point_summary: David's example in 1 Chronicles 29 illustrates that joyful, willing giving flows from recognizing that everything offered to God already belongs to Him.

context: David's charge to his son Solomon before his death.
point_summary: God's command to Joshua — "be strong and of a good courage" — frames David's deathbed charge to Solomon in 1 Chronicles 28 as a parallel call to courageous faithfulness passed from one generation to the next.

context: And David said to Solomon his son, Be strong and of a good courage, and do it. Fear not, nor be dismayed. For the Lord God, even my God, will be with thee. I will not fail thee, nor forsake thee, until thou hast finished all the work for the service of the house of the Lord.
point_summary: David's deathbed charge to Solomon — be strong, courageous, and unafraid — reveals that David understood courage to be grounded in God's promised presence and faithfulness to complete the work He assigns.

context: What is it that David said to Solomon in 1 Chronicles 28 and verse number 20? He tells him to be courageous, but he says, the Lord will be with you.
point_summary: Knowing that God is with you is the source of courage, as David's own words to Solomon in 1 Chronicles 28:20 confirm.

context: ...And he, David, appointed certain of the Levites to minister before the ark of the Lord, and to record, and to thank and praise the Lord God of Israel.
point_summary: David's deliberate appointment of Levites to minister, record, thank, and praise establishes that corporate worship of God is a structured, intentional priority in Israel's life under his leadership.

context: ...Then on that day David delivered first this psalm to thank the Lord into the hand of Asaph and his brethren.
point_summary: On the day the ark was restored to Jerusalem, David's *first* act was to deliver a psalm of thanksgiving, signaling that praise held deliberate priority in Israel's national worship.

context: Give thanks unto the Lord. Call upon His name. Make known His deeds among the people.
point_summary: At a pinnacle moment in Israel's history, David's first priority upon returning the ark to Jerusalem was corporate thanksgiving and praise, as signaled by the word "first" in verse 7.

context: Sing unto Him. Sing psalms unto Him. Talk ye of all His wondrous works.
point_summary: Singing praise to God held deliberate priority in David's worship at the ark's return, as the psalm he delivered "first" called Israel to sing, declare His works, and glory in His name.

context: Glory in His holy name. Let the heart of them rejoice.
point_summary: Glorying in God's holy name is presented as the fitting response of hearts that seek Him, set within David's psalm of praise at the ark's arrival in Jerusalem.

context: Seek the Lord. Seek the Lord and His strength. Seek His face continually.
point_summary: Seeking the Lord and His strength continually is presented as the defining posture of David's psalm of praise at the ark's return to Jerusalem.

context: ...For great is the Lord and greatly to be praised. He also is to be feared above all gods.
point_summary: God's greatness is the stated ground for praise in David's psalm, cited here as the theological foundation for Israel's corporate worship at the ark's return to Jerusalem.

context: For all the gods of the people are idols, but the Lord made the heavens.
point_summary: All the gods of the nations are mere idols, but the Lord is distinguished as the one who made the heavens, establishing His unique worthiness to be praised above all others.

context: ...Give unto the Lord the glory due unto His name. Bring an offering and come before Him. Worship the Lord in the beauty of holiness...
point_summary: Worship owed to God's name is inseparable from bringing an offering and coming before Him, as David's psalm frames praise as the fitting response to witnessing God's mighty acts.

context: ...for before Him, all the earth, the world also shall be stable, that it be not moved...
point_summary: The stability of the earth before God ("the world also shall be stable, that it be not moved") grounds the call to universal praise and rejoicing voiced throughout David's psalm.

context: ...Let the heavens be glad, and let the earth rejoice, and let men say among the nations, The Lord reigneth...
point_summary: Cosmic creation itself is called to rejoice in the Lord's reign, situating praise as the fitting response of all the earth—not merely Israel—to God's sovereign rule.

context: ...Let the sea roar, and the fullness thereof...
point_summary: Creation's participation in praise — sea, fields, and woods — frames the call for all the earth to rejoice and declare the Lord's reign, situating human thanksgiving within a cosmic chorus of worship.

context: ...Let the fields rejoice, and all that is therein. Then shall the trees of the woods sing out at the presence of the Lord, because He cometh to judge the earth...
point_summary: Creation's joyful song at the Lord's coming to judge the earth is cited as part of David's psalm establishing a priority of praise in response to God's mighty works.

context: ...Oh, give thanks unto the Lord for He is good. For His mercy endureth forever...
point_summary: God's enduring goodness and mercy, voiced in David's psalm at the ark's installation, are presented as the proper ground and motive for thanksgiving.

context: ...And say ye, Save us, O God, of our salvation, and gather us together, and deliver us from the heathen, that we may give thanks to Thy holy name, and glory in Thy praise...
point_summary: Salvation from enemies and deliverance from the nations are given as the very purpose for which God's people ask to be gathered—so that they may give thanks and glory in His praise.

context: ...Blessed be the Lord God of Israel forever and ever. And all the people said, Amen, and praised the Lord...
point_summary: The congregational "Amen" and praise that closes David's psalm in verse 36 illustrates the fitting response to God's mighty works—and sets the standard that believers should praise Him not only in deliverance but also in adversity.

context: I believe that 1 Chronicles chapter 29 is probably within the whole of scripture, the greatest illustration of someone that's giving cheerfully, and that's David.
point_summary: 1 Chronicles 29 is cited as Scripture's greatest illustration of cheerful giving, with David's example notable because he gave generously toward a temple he would never personally use or worship in.

context: if you understand within the context of 1 Chronicles 29, that David was giving for a temple that he would never see
point_summary: 1 Chronicles 29 is cited as Scripture's greatest illustration of cheerful giving, with David's example made striking by the fact that he gave generously toward a temple he would never personally worship in.

context: I won't take you there for the sake of time, but if we went to 1 Chronicles 15, verse 17, we would learn that Ethan, of course, is a Levite, and Ethan was put in charge of worship.
point_summary: Ethan the Ezraite's identification in 1 Chronicles 15:17 as a Levite appointed over worship establishes his authoritative credentials as the author of Psalm 89, a psalm designed to instruct God's people in his faithfulness.

context: Glory ye in his holy name. Let the heart of them rejoice that seek the Lord.
point_summary: Revival is rightly understood as a renewed seeking of the Lord, and 1 Chronicles 16:10 grounds that seeking in joy — the heart of those who seek Him rejoices.

context: And it was because the Bible tells us that there was sin in the camp. It's very interesting if you were to take the time, we don't have the time this morning, but if you were to study through Joshua chapter number seven and you would find that that sin in the camp, it was Achan, the troubler of Israel as he's referred to in the book of First Chronicles.
point_summary: Achan's individual choice to take the accursed thing—recorded in 1 Chronicles as "the troubler of Israel"—illustrates how one person's decision can devastate an entire congregation, costing 36 lives at Ai.

context: the perspective from 1 Chronicles 29
point_summary: Wealth ultimately belongs to God, who graciously gives it to his people and then grants them the privilege of returning it to him for his honor and glory.

context: Achan is described in 1 Chronicles in the genealogies that no one likes to read? He is the troubler of Israel.
point_summary: Achan's label as "troubler of Israel" in the Chronicles genealogies illustrates how unconfessed, hidden sin carries lasting consequences that ultimately become publicly known.

context: he's referred to in 1 Chronicles as being the troubler of Israel
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 1 Chronicles chapter 29
point_summary: 1 Chronicles 29 is presented as a premier biblical illustration of cheerful giving, introduced to embody in narrative form the principle Paul states in 2 Corinthians 9:7.

context: here in 1 Chronicles 29 that God illustrates for us beautifully
point_summary: First Chronicles 29 is presented as the Bible's premier illustration of cheerful, willing giving, shown in David's wholehearted preparation for a temple he would never personally see or use.

context: he says in verse 2, I have prepared with all my might
point_summary: David's wholehearted, self-expending preparation for a temple he would never personally use illustrates the willing, joyful generosity that 2 Corinthians 9:7 calls for.

context: the Bible says in verse number 7, and gave for the service of the house of God
point_summary: David's lavish, willing offering for a temple he would never see illustrates the cheerful, voluntary giving commended in 2 Corinthians 9:7, grounded in the recognition that God alone enables wealth.

context: verse number 9, then the people rejoiced, for that they offered willingly, because with perfect heart, they offered willingly to the Lord, and David the king also rejoiced with great joy
point_summary: Willing, joyful giving—illustrated by Israel's offering for the temple—is held up as the Old Testament picture of what Paul commands in 2 Corinthians 9:7: giving not grudgingly but from the heart.

context: verse number 10, David begins to speak, and it says, wherefore David, bless the Lord before all the congregation
point_summary: David's doxology in verse 10 grounds cheerful giving in the recognition that all wealth and honor originate with God, making generosity a response of praise rather than a sacrifice of one's own resources.

context: And thou reignest over all, and in thine hand is power and might, and in thine hand it is to make great and to give strength unto all
point_summary: Wealth and the ability to give both originate entirely with God, so that giving back to Him is simply returning what was already His — a truth David's prayer makes explicit.

context: but who am I and what is my people that we should be able to offer so willingly after this sort for all things
point_summary: Giving back to God is an act of humble acknowledgment that everything offered already belongs to Him — "of thine own have we given thee" — making generosity an act of worship, not ownership.

context: For we are strangers before thee and sojourners, as were all our fathers. Our days on the earth are as a shadow, and there is none abiding
point_summary: Human transience on earth — "our days on the earth are as a shadow" — grounds David's gratitude: since all things come from God, giving back to Him is simply returning what was already His.

context: O Lord, our God, all the store that we have prepared to build thee in house for thine holy name cometh of thine hand and is all thine own
point_summary: Everything Israel offered to build the temple was already God's own — a truth David voiced to reframe giving not as sacrifice but as returning what came from God's hand.

context: if you were to pick it up and look in the text of Scripture in 1 Chronicles, and just kind of midpoint
point_summary: David's joyful giving toward a temple he would never enter stands as a scriptural model of cheerful, God-enabled generosity offered for the benefit of a future generation.
